sanskrit_devanagari:
अधियज्ञः कथं कोऽत्र देहेऽस्मिन्मधुसूदन ।
प्रयाणकाले च कथं ज्ञेयोऽसि नियतात्मभिः ॥ २ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
adhiyajñaḥ kathaṁ ko 'tra dehe 'smin madhusūdana |
prayāṇa-kāle ca kathaṁ jñeyo 'si niyatātmabhiḥ || 2 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_08.002 = MBh_06,030.002).

anvaya.meaning: the Lord of sacrifice / the principle presiding over the offering
at the time of departure / time of death
by those of self-controlled / disciplined selves

translations.verbatim_quote: Who is the Lord of sacrifice, and how does He live in the body, O Madhusūdana? And how can those engaged in devotional service know You at the time of death?
Who is adhiyajña (the Lord of sacrifice), and how does he dwell in this body, O Madhusudan? And how can those of steadfast mind know you at the time of death?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Lord of sacrifice may be Indra or Viṣṇu. Viṣṇu is the chief of the principal demigods, including Brahmā and Śiva, and Indra is the chief of the administrative demigods. Both Indra and Viṣṇu are worshiped by yajña performances.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.2 Who, what entity, is the adhiyajña, in this body, and how does he abide here? Prayana-kale, at the time of death; and how (katham), in what manner; jneyah asi, are You to be known; niyata-atmabhih, by those of controlled minds — by the disciplined ones?
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the seven questions as a doctrinally bundled unit and addresses them at 8.2. The adhiyajña question is the structural pivot — who presides over the offering in this very body — and prepares the ground for Krishna's answer at 8.4 (adhiyajño 'ham eva atra dehe). The 'how to be known at the time of death' question (prayāṇa-kāle ... jñeyo 'si) sets up the chapter's central practice (8.5-8.13).

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.1– 8.2 Who is Adhiyajna that is to be known by the three groups as their dying hour. In what manner are You to be known by these three groups who are self-controlled?
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja groups 8.1-8.2 and reads the adhiyajña question as the pivot for all three classes of aspirant: release-seekers, wealth-seekers, and the self-controlled (niyatātmabhiḥ) all need to know who the adhiyajña is in the body and how the Lord is to be known at the dying hour. The seventh question is the chapter's central practical question.

positions.rendering: the Lord of sacrifice (later identified as Krishna himself in the body, per 8.4)
the entity presiding over the offering, abiding in this very body — Krishna himself per 8.4
the inner principle to be known by all three classes of aspirant at the dying hour

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: adhiyajña is one of the canonical seven technical terms. All four traditions agree that Krishna's answer at 8.4 (adhiyajño 'ham eva atra dehe — 'I am the adhiyajña here in the body') resolves the question. The question itself, posed at 8.2, frames the structural pivot of the chapter: the Lord-of-the-offering is not external to the body but resides within it. Engineering analog cannot translate this term; the source pack preserves it verbatim.

positions.rendering: those of controlled minds, the disciplined ones
the self-controlled aspirants across all three groups

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: niyatātmabhiḥ = 'by those whose self/mind is restrained or disciplined'. Prabhupada specifies devotional discipline; Shankara and Mukundananda preserve the more general 'restrained mind'; Ramanuja scopes it to all three classes of aspirant the chapter addresses. The Sanskrit force is the prerequisite — only those who have already done the discipline can know-the-Lord at the time of death. This is the chapter's claim about who the practice is available to.

literal_meaning: Who is the adhiyajña, and how is he here in this body, O Madhusūdana? And how are you to be known at the time of departure by those of disciplined selves?
traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna completes his seven canonical questions: (6) who is the adhiyajña — the Lord-of-the-offering — and how does that principle reside in this very body; (7) how is Krishna to be known at the time of death (prayāṇa-kāle) by those whose minds have been disciplined (niyatātmabhiḥ). The adhiyajña question pivots the chapter inward: the offering's presider is not external. The dying-hour question pivots the chapter forward: the rest of the chapter (8.5-8.13) gives the practice. Shankara and Ramanuja both treat 8.1-8.2 as a single doctrinal unit; their bhasyas address all seven questions together. The vocative madhusūdana (slayer of Madhu) names Krishna's Vishnu-aspect — Arjuna addresses the cosmic deity, not just the friend in the chariot.
